citation: Naaijer, Martijn, and Dirk Roorda. 2016. "Parallel Texts in the Hebrew Bible, New Methods and Visualizations." arXiv:1603.01541. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.1603.01541.
research_field: computational biblical studies; digital humanities; text reuse
corpus: Complete Masoretic Text from ETCBC/BHSA; separate MT Isaiah–1QIsaa visualization
method: All-by-all chunk comparison; lexeme-set intersection-over-union; Levenshtein/LCS similarity; clique thresholding; synoptic visualization
benchmark: Well-known Hebrew Bible parallels and 2 Kings 19–25 case study; no formal held-out gold set reported
evaluation_method: 165 parameter experiments; automatic clique/longest-clique heuristic; qualitative recovery and miss analysis
expert_review: Scholarly qualitative review by authors; no independent blinded adjudication reported
data_available: yes: ETCBC repository, annotations, output, and archived data
claimed_novelty: Authors present an open-source online tool for detecting and synoptically visualizing Hebrew Bible parallels
limitations: Lexical only; semantic and order-based parallels missed; threshold and chunk choices define results; formulaic and drifting cliques
relevance_to_project_echoes: Closest transparent precedent for undirected all-pairs full-Hebrew-Bible lexical search; lacks GNT, independent detector families, knownness workflow, and persistent rejection ledger
notes: Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/1603.01541 | Project: https://github.com/ETCBC/parallels | Data DOI: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.1007643

citation: McGovern, Hope, Hale Sirin, Tom Lippincott, and Andrew Caines. 2024. "Detecting Narrative Patterns in Biblical Hebrew and Greek." Proceedings of the 1st Workshop on Machine Learning for Ancient Languages, 269–279. https://doi.org/10.18653/v1/2024.ml4al-1.26.
research_field: computational narrative; biblical studies; information retrieval
corpus: Leningrad Codex Hebrew Bible; Greek LXX plus SBLGNT; OpenBible cross-references
textual_unit: query verse with surrounding context; candidate verse or chapter
method: Siamese BERT-like encoders; contrastive triplet-margin training on cross-reference pairs and random negatives; dot-product retrieval
training_data: OpenBible cross-reference pairs with vote threshold >=5; random negative verses; 90/10 train-validation split; pretrained Hebrew and Greek encoders
benchmark: 17,899 Hebrew and 45,297 Greek cross-reference pairs; nine manually constructed type-scene queries
evaluation_method: Recall@k from 1 to 50; ablations for model, masking, batch size, and context window; manual error analysis
expert_review: Language expert helped craft Hebrew queries; Greek queries were LLM-generated then manually verified; no comprehensive independent type-scene gold set
code_available: uncertain: paper claims public code but prints non-actionable anonymous/repo URL
data_available: partial: source corpora and OpenBible links are public; processed splits were not verified
claimed_novelty: Authors present a novel cross-reference-trained retrieval approach for extracting biblical type-scenes
limitations: Low recall; random negatives; single verses often too small; Greek query generation used an LLM; crowd-reference and translation circularity; reported Greek corpus has 64 books
relevance_to_project_echoes: Direct narrative-detector precedent across Hebrew and Greek; unlike Echoes it is query-directed, weakly supervised by known links, and lacks independent evidence-family agreement

citation: McGovern, Hope, Hale Sirin, and Tom Lippincott. 2025. "Computational Discovery of Chiasmus in Ancient Religious Text." Proceedings of NAACL-HLT 2025, Volume 2: Short Papers, 154–160. https://doi.org/10.18653/v1/2025.naacl-short.13.
research_field: computational rhetoric; biblical studies; structural pattern detection
corpus: Translator's Amalgamated Hebrew Old Testament based on the Leningrad Codex
phenomenon: chiasmus; chiastic and non-chiastic structural repetition
textual_unit: half-verse and verse; sliding windows of 4–8 units
method: Multilingual E5-small embeddings; cosine similarity matrix; paired-versus-nonpaired structural score; corpus z-score threshold
supervised_or_unsupervised: unsupervised detector with post-hoc human evaluation
training_data: No task-specific training; pretrained multilingual E5
benchmark: Top 50 half-verse and top 50 verse candidates manually annotated into three classes
evaluation_method: Precision@k 0.60 half-verse and 0.80 verse; Cohen kappa 0.76 and 0.89; qualitative distribution analysis
expert_review: Two author-annotators with graduate-level ancient-language and literature training
data_available: yes: repository outputs and formatted STEP Bible dataset DOI 10.57967/hf/4174
claimed_novelty: Authors claim the first systematic computational approach and mathematical formalization for biblical chiasmus detection
limitations: Hebrew OT only; verse and half-verse structures only; no narrative-level chiasmus; top-k evaluation cannot measure recall; formulaic repetition is frequent
relevance_to_project_echoes: Strong precedent for Echoes' structural detector, but not cross-passage whole-Hebrew-plus-Greek discovery or a multi-family ensemble
notes: Paper: https://aclanthology.org/2025.naacl-short.13/ | DOI: https://doi.org/10.18653/v1/2025.naacl-short.13 | Code: https://github.com/comp-int-hum/literary-translation | Dataset DOI: https://doi.org/10.57967/hf/4174

citation: Smiley, David M. 2025. "Intertextual Parallel Detection in Biblical Hebrew: A Transformer-Based Benchmark." arXiv:2506.24117v2. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2506.24117.
research_field: computational biblical Hebrew; natural-language processing; semantic retrieval
corpus: BHSA; 558 recognized Chronicles verses with parallels in Samuel/Kings
phenomenon: known intertextual and synoptic parallel passages
method: E5, AlephBERT, MPNet, and LaBSE verse embeddings; cosine similarity; Wasserstein distribution distance; nearest-neighbor classification
supervised_or_unsupervised: zero-shot task evaluation using externally pretrained models
training_data: No task-specific training; pretrained transformer corpora, including Modern Hebrew for AlephBERT
benchmark: 558 known parallel verses; all other Samuel/Kings verses used as non-parallel comparisons
evaluation_method: Parallel/non-parallel cosine distributions; t-tests; Wasserstein distance; nearest-neighbor precision, recall, and F1; E5 F1 0.88 and AlephBERT F1 0.87
expert_review: Uses a published scholarly parallel synopsis; no new independent expert adjudication reported
claimed_novelty: Authors claim the first comprehensive transformer-model benchmark for detecting biblical parallels
limitations: Restricted to known narrative parallels; other genres untested; embeddings opaque; models not Biblical-Hebrew-specific; unlisted verses may include unlabeled positives
relevance_to_project_echoes: Useful precedent for Echoes' semantic benchmark and hard-negative design; not whole-canon, undirected, interpretable, or multi-family discovery
notes: Preprint: https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.24117 | DOI: https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2506.24117 | Code/data: https://github.com/dmsmiley/detect-bh

citation: de la Selle, Théotime, and Laurence Mellerin. 2026. "Detection and Typology of Psalmic Text Reuses in the New Testament." Religions 17(1):88. https://doi.org/10.3390/rel17010088.
research_field: computational intertextuality; ancient Greek NLP; text reuse
corpus: 7,939 Greek New Testament verses; 3,093 manually segmented LXX Psalm verse-parts; 24,555,327 possible pairs; 614-item initial gold standard
phenomenon: Psalm quotations, allusions, echoes, coincidences, and thematic reuse in the New Testament
textual_unit: New Testament verse paired with Psalm verse-part
method: Eight representations—tokens, lemmas, POS, stop words, filtered lemmas, synonym lexicon, semantic domains, and subdomains—under two normalizations; Levenshtein and custom set distance; ranking and K-means clustering
training_data: none; gold standard reserved for validation and interpretation
benchmark: 614 literature-derived, manually typologized reuses, later augmented to 682 after reviewing detector output
evaluation_method: Top-240 recovery against gold and Passim; source-verse prediction rates; K-means typology coherence; manual review of proposed additions
expert_review: Extensive literature-derived gold set; manual Psalm segmentation, tagging, semantic-domain completion, typology, and top-result review
data_available: yes: code, processed representations, and datasets in project repository
claimed_novelty: Authors introduce a transparent literal/grammatical/lexical/semantic pair model and report 68 possible previously unpublished reuses; they also state none of the new detections is useful for exegetical or theological analysis
limitations: Psalms-to-NT only; Septuagint variants excluded; patchwork resources and substantial manual completion; weak implicit-echo performance; detector-derived gold augmentation risks non-independent evaluation
relevance_to_project_echoes: Closest multi-representation original-Greek intrabiblical detector and limited known-link check; lacks full corpus, Hebrew triangulation, independent detector-family agreement, and broad knownness workflow
